summary: Atwood argues that while rigid hierarchical categorization systems like Yahoo's directory model are obsolete, hierarchies still have tremendous value as filters for disambiguating search results. Using the example of searching for 'Jaguar' (which could mean the car, OS X version, animal, or Atari console), he contrasts Google's flat search results with eBay's category-based filtering that lets users narrow results with a single click. He proposes that Google could automatically suggest semantic disambiguations similar to how they suggest spelling corrections, potentially using Markov chain analysis of existing web pages to identify common meanings.
key_points: Single-word searches carry semantic ambiguity that exists only in the user's head, not in the search box
While rigid hierarchical directories are obsolete ('evil'), hierarchies remain powerful as semantic-narrowing filters on search results
eBay's category filtering demonstrates how users can disambiguate with minimal effort compared to typing additional keywords
Google could extend its 'Did you mean...' feature beyond spelling corrections to semantic disambiguation
Such a disambiguation system could potentially be automated using Markov chain probabilities rather than manual categorization
money_quotes: The semantics of Jaguar only exist in my head, not in any search box.
My apologies to Yet Another Hierarchically Organized Oracle and the Open Directory Project, but rigid hierarchy is evil.
A rigid hierarchy is tremendously powerful as a semantic-narrowing filter on search results.
A single word should get me what I want.
If Google is going to suggest 'Did you mean...' corrections when I misspell a search term, why don't they do the same thing to disambiguate semantics?
In a brave new Google world of 'I'll just type in what I want and hit Enter' search, there may still be room for some quasi-evil hierarchy in there somewhere.
key_insight: Hierarchies aren't inherently evil—they become powerful tools when used as optional filters to narrow search results rather than as rigid gatekeepers to content.
